text: During laparoscopy, the surgeon observes a torsion of the right ovarian cyst with gangrene. The video shows a large cyst located in the fimbria, with the right fallopian tube and ovary twisted into two to three knots.
The surgeon gently maneuvers to untwist the torsion, revealing the twisted right fallopian tube. The ureter is also visible, showing clear peristalsis.

After detorsion, the surgeon inspects the uterus and the left ovary, which appear normal. Using bipolar diathermy, the gangrenous section of the right fallopian tube and ovary is dissected and removed.

The surgeon uses the left hand to dissect the gangrenous right fallopian tube and ovary, focusing on dividing the pedicle while maneuvering on the right side. The large ovary and femoral cyst are visible, showing signs of gangrene.
The surgeon carefully completes the division of the gangrenous fallopian tube and ovarian pedicle using diathermy, ensuring no injury occurs to the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon coagulates visible bleeding points in the surgical field, completing the easier part of the procedure. The left ovary and uterus are inspected for any abnormalities.
The surgeon aspirates a serous cyst, extracting approximately fifty to eighty milliliters of clear serous fluid.

The surgeon positions the ovary and cyst within an endobag for retrieval. Hemostasis is ensured, and excess fluid is suctioned out before the entire cyst and ovary are removed through the umbilical port using an endovac.
